questions.q: Radioactive decay is described as spontaneous and random. Which statement follows from this?
options: The decay rate can be sped up by heating the sample
External conditions (temperature, pressure, chemistry) have no effect on the decay rate, and the time of decay of a single nucleus cannot be predicted
Every nucleus decays after exactly one half-life
Compressing the sample shortens its half-life
questions.explanation: Spontaneous means the rate is unaffected by temperature, pressure or chemical state — half-life is a fixed property of the nucleus. Random means you cannot predict when any individual nucleus will decay; only the statistics of huge numbers of nuclei are predictable.

options: Because nuclei decay at a constant number per second regardless of how many remain
Because the rate of decay is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present, so the rate falls as the population falls
Because the decay constant doubles each half-life
questions.explanation: The decay rate is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei. As nuclei are used up the rate drops, which slows the using-up further — a quantity whose rate of decrease is proportional to itself is exactly exponential decay: \(N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}\).

options: The time for the entire sample to decay
The time for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay (equivalently, for the activity to fall to half its value)
The time for the decay constant to halve
Half the total lifetime of a single nucleus
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay, or equivalently for the activity to fall to half. It is NOT the time for the whole sample to disappear — after each half-life only half decays, so the amount keeps halving and never quite reaches zero.

questions.q: After 3 half-lives, what fraction of the original sample remains?
questions.explanation: After \(n\) half-lives the fraction remaining is \(\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n\). For \(n=3\): \(\tfrac{1}{2}\to\tfrac{1}{4}\to\tfrac{1}{8}\). A common error is to assume the sample is gone after a few half-lives, but an eighth still remains.

questions.q: How is the decay constant \(\lambda\) related to the half-life \(t_{1/2}\)?
questions.explanation: \(\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}\). The two are inversely related: a large decay constant (nuclei likely to decay) means a short half-life, and vice versa.

questions.q: What is the difference between the 'amount remaining' and the 'activity' of a source?
options: They are the same quantity measured in different units
Amount remaining (N) is how many undecayed nuclei/grams are left; activity (R, in becquerels) is the number of decays per second — different quantities that share the same half-life
Activity is measured in grams; amount remaining is measured in becquerels
questions.explanation: The amount remaining counts undecayed nuclei (or mass); the activity is decays per second (becquerels). They follow the same exponential law and halve together, but answer different questions — read the verb and give the matching quantity.

questions.q: A source has an activity of 8 MBq and a half-life of 10 minutes. How long until its activity falls to 1 MBq?
questions.explanation: Count the halvings: 8 → 4 (one), 4 → 2 (two), 2 → 1 (three). That is 3 half-lives × 10 minutes = 30 minutes.

questions.q: A 20 mg sample of iodine-123 has a half-life of 13 hours. Roughly how much remains after 24 hours? (\(\lambda = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}\), \(N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}\))
questions.explanation: \(\lambda = 0.693/13 = 0.0533\ \mathrm{h^{-1}}\); exponent \(= -0.0533\times24 = -1.279\); \(e^{-1.279} \approx 0.278\); \(N = 20\times0.278 \approx 5.6\ \mathrm{mg}\). (24 h is 1.85 half-lives, so a bit more than a quarter of 20 mg remains.) Always find \(\lambda\) first and keep time units consistent.

questions.q: The activity of a source falls from 600 counts/min to 100 counts/min over 6 hours. What is the decay constant, with correct units?
questions.explanation: \(100 = 600\,e^{-6\lambda} \Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{6} = e^{-6\lambda} \Rightarrow \ln(\tfrac{1}{6}) = -1.792 = -6\lambda \Rightarrow \lambda = 0.299\). Because the time was in hours, \(\lambda\) is in per hour (\(\mathrm{h^{-1}}\)), not per second — \(\lambda\) and \(t\) must carry the same time unit.

questions.q: Why is a gamma-emitting isotope with a half-life of a few hours suitable as a diagnostic medical tracer, while an alpha emitter is not?
options: Gamma is highly ionising so it images well; alpha is too penetrating

Gamma penetrates the body so it reaches the external detector and is weakly ionising (gentle on tissue), and the half-life is long enough to image yet short enough to clear; alpha is absorbed inside the body (undetectable externally) and strongly ionising (damaging)
Alpha is undetectable because it has no charge
Gamma has a longer half-life than alpha so it lasts longer in the body
questions.explanation: 'Goldilocks and the Ghost': the half-life must be long enough to inject, travel and scan, yet short enough to clear and minimise dose. Gamma is highly penetrating (escapes to the gamma camera) and weakly ionising (low tissue damage). Alpha is strongly ionising and barely penetrating, so it cannot be detected externally and deposits a damaging dose internally.
